1.7: hoti polloi planoi exelthon eis ton kosmon hoi me homologountes Iesoun Christon erchomenon en sarki houtos estin ho planos kai ho antichristos (hoti polloi planoi exēlthon eis ton kosmon, hoi mē homologountes Iēsoun Christon erchomenon en sarki): 'For many deceivers have gone out into the world, those who do not confess the coming of Jesus Christ in the flesh.' The present participle erchomenon (coming) rather than the perfect elēlythota (having come, 1 John 4:2) may signal a future reference — not the Incarnation but the parousia. Either way, the test is the same: affirm the bodily reality of Jesus's existence, whether past (Incarnation) or future (Return). The Docetic denial of Christ's flesh eliminates both the atonement and the resurrection-body hope.
1.1: Ho presbyteros eklekte kyria kai tois teknois autes (Ho presbyteros eklektē kyria kai tois teknois autēs): 'The elder to the elect lady and her children.' Ho presbyteros (the elder) without a personal name is distinctive — the definite article implies a unique individual recognized in the region by this title alone. Papias (preserved in Eusebius HE 3.39) distinguishes between 'John the Apostle' and 'John the Elder' of Asia — the author of 2 and 3 John identifies by this title only. Eklektē kyria (elect lady) — kyria is the feminine of kyrios; whether a personal name (Kyria) or a metaphor for a local congregation (as 1 Pet 5:13 uses 'she who is in Babylon' for a church) is debated. The personification of a congregation as a woman draws on the OT imagery of Zion/Jerusalem as daughter and bride. Eklektē (elect, chosen) echoes 1 Pet 2:9's 'elect race' — the election-language applied to the community.
1.2: dia ten aletheian ten menousan en hemin (dia tēn alētheian tēn menousan en hēmin): 'because of the truth that abides in us.' Aletheia (truth) in the Johannine letters is not primarily propositional but personal and indwelling — the Spirit of truth who 'abides' (menei) in the community (cf. John 14:17: 'the Spirit of truth... dwells with you and will be in you'; 1 John 2:27: the anointing 'abides in you'). Menō (abide/remain) is the signature Johannine word for divine indwelling, appearing approximately 40 times in John's Gospel; its appearance here signals that the truth at stake is not an abstract doctrine but the living presence of the Spirit-of-truth within the community. The phrase 'will be with us forever' (met hemon estai eis ton aiōna) gives the truth an eschatological permanence that relativizes the false teachers' claim to 'progress' beyond it (v. 9).
1.3: estai meth hemon charis eleos eirene para theou patros kai para Iesou Christou tou huiou tou patros en aletheia kai agape: 'Grace, mercy, and peace will be with us from God the Father and from Jesus Christ the Father's Son, in truth and love.' The triple greeting (grace/charis, mercy/eleos, peace/eirene) is the expanded Pastoral Epistles form (1 Tim 1:2; 2 Tim 1:2) rather than Paul's standard 'grace and peace.' Eleos (mercy) is the LXX rendering of Hebrew hesed (covenant loyalty/steadfast love) — its appearance alongside grace and peace grounds the letter's community-concern in divine covenant faithfulness. The indicative 'will be' (estai) rather than an optative wish ('may be') may signal confidence in divine provision. The double source 'from God the Father and from Jesus Christ' places Father and Son as co-equal origins of the apostolic blessing.
1.4: echaren lian hoti heureka ek ton teknon sou peripatountas en aletheia (echarēn lian hoti heurēka ek tōn teknōn sou peripatountas en alētheia): 'I rejoiced greatly to find some of your children walking in the truth.' The elder 'found' (heurēka, perfect tense: a discovery with lasting significance) 'some' (ek tōn, from among the children) — not all — walking in truth, implying others are deviating. Peripatountas en aletheia (walking in truth) is the Johannine ethical idiom: the whole-life embodiment of the gospel (cf. 3 John 3-4; 1 John 1:6-7, 2:6; John 8:12). Walking is the OT halakha-imagery (the walk of the righteous vs. wicked, Ps 1:1) — truth as a path to walk, not merely a proposition to affirm. 'Just as we were commanded by the Father' grounds the ethical norm in divine command rather than community convention.
1.5: kai nun eroto se kyria ouchi hos entolen kainen graphon soi alla hen eichomen ap arches hina agapomen allelous: 'And now I ask you, dear lady — not as though I were writing you a new commandment, but the one we have had from the beginning — that we love one another.' The 'not new but from the beginning' formula appears verbatim in 1 John 2:7 — indicating an established formulaic tradition in the Johannine community. The commandment to 'love one another' is Jesus's own word (John 13:34: 'A new commandment I give you, that you love one another') — paradoxically described as 'not new' because it is foundational rather than a recent addition. The Johannine community's identity was constituted by this command; its 'from the beginning' status makes it impossible to 'progress beyond' (v. 9) without abandoning the community's defining reality.
1.6: kai haute estin he agape hina peripatomen kata tas entolas autou haute he entole estin kathos ekousate ap arches hina en aute peripatete: 'And this is love, that we walk according to his commandments. This is the commandment, just as you have heard from the beginning, that you should walk in it.' The circular definition — love = keeping commandments; commandment = love — is a deliberate Johannine tautology that closes two possible escape routes: (1) antinomian love (claiming to love without ethical content); (2) loveless legalism (commandments without the animating affection). The spiral structure (love → commandments → love → commandments) reflects the Johannine community's understanding that love and obedience are not two things but one reality seen from different angles. 'Just as you have heard from the beginning' again invokes the apostolic tradition as the standard — the same tradition the false teachers' 'going ahead' (v. 9) would leave behind.
1.8: blepete heautous hina me apolesete ha eirgazametha alla misthon plere apolabesthe: 'Watch yourselves, so that you may not lose what we have worked for, but may win a full reward.' Blepete heautous (watch/look to yourselves) — the reflexive imperative for communal self-examination and doctrinal vigilance. The textual variants in 'we/you have worked' and 'we/you receive' reflect early scribal uncertainty about the referent: is the work the apostolic labor (we worked) or the community's faith-walk (you worked)? Either way, the point is that the gains of apostolic mission can be forfeited through doctrinal defection. Misthon plere (full reward) — the concept of eschatological reward in the NT is consistently judgment-day in orientation: the outcome of faithful perseverance will be declared at the parousia (Rev 22:12; Matt 16:27). The implicit contrast: those who 'go ahead' (v. 9) beyond Christ's teaching receive neither the Father nor a reward.
1.9: pas ho proagon kai me menon en te didache tou Christou theon ouk echei ho menon en te didache houtos kai ton patera kai ton huion echei: 'Everyone who goes ahead (proagōn) and does not abide in the teaching of Christ does not have God. Whoever abides in the teaching has both the Father and the Son.' Proagō (go ahead, progress beyond) is the false teachers' self-description: they claim to 'advance' beyond the basic gospel to deeper or higher teaching. The elder's counter-claim subverts the language entirely: to go ahead of Christ's teaching is not progress but apostasy — the one who leaves the teaching loses the teacher and the Father along with him. Menōn en te didachē (abiding in the teaching) applies the signature Johannine menō-language to doctrinal fidelity: just as the believer must abide in Christ and Christ's words must abide in the believer (John 15:4-7), the teaching of Christ must be the community's permanent dwelling-place.
1.10: ei tis erchetai pros hymas kai tauten ten didachen ou pherei me lambaneite auton eis oikian kai chairein auto me legete: 'If anyone comes to you and does not bring this teaching, do not receive him into your house or give him any greeting.' The house (oikia) was the church's meeting-place in the NT period — to 'receive into the house' was to give a traveling teacher access to the assembled congregation, hospitality, and the community's endorsement. Mediterranean honor-culture imposed an almost absolute obligation of hospitality on householders; refusing a traveler was a severe breach of social custom. The instruction to withhold both house-access and greeting (chairein) is thus radically counter-cultural — but the false teacher's exploitation of the hospitality-network is precisely how the deception spreads. The doctrinal boundary here is a practical mechanism for protecting the community's gathering-space from infiltration.
1.11: ho gar legon auto chairein koinonei tois ergois autou tois ponerois: 'For whoever greets him takes part in his wicked works.' Chairein (greetings!, literally 'rejoice!') was the standard epistolary and personal greeting of the Hellenistic world — the first word of most Hellenistic letters. To give the formal greeting was a public act of social endorsement: it declared the greeter's solidarity with and approval of the person greeted. Koinōnei (shares in, participates in, has fellowship with) — the same root as koinōnia (fellowship/communion) — means that greeting the false teacher makes one a partner (koinōnos) in his 'wicked works.' The Johannine ethical logic: social acts (greeting, receiving) carry theological weight; they are not neutral courtesies but declarations of communal alignment.
1.12: polla echon hymin graphein ouk eboulethyn dia chartou kai melanos alla elpizo genesthai pros hymas kai stoma pros stoma lalein hina he chara hemon pepleromene e: 'Though I have much to write to you, I would rather not use paper and ink. Instead, I hope to come to you and talk face to face (stoma pros stoma), so that our joy may be complete.' The convention of preferring personal presence to written communication is standard in Hellenistic epistolography (Plato Ep. 7; Cicero; Seneca). Chartes kai melas (papyrus and ink) — chartes is specifically papyrus-paper (Gr. loan from Egyptian), not the generic 'paper'; melas (black) is the ink. Stoma pros stoma (mouth to mouth, face to face) — the most intimate form of personal communication (cf. Num 12:8: YHWH speaks with Moses 'mouth to mouth'). Given the letter's concern with the bodily reality of Christ (v. 7), the insistence on bodily presence for true fellowship enacts the Johannine principle: the Incarnation validates embodied encounter as the highest form of communication.
1.13: aspazontai se ta tekna tes adelphes sou tes eklektes: 'The children of your elect sister greet you.' The 'elect sister' is the companion congregation in the elder's community — the personified-church metaphor maintained consistently through the letter's close (cf. 1 Pet 5:13: 'She who is at Babylon, who is likewise chosen, sends you greetings'). The communal greeting (from the children of one church to another church's children) reveals the Johannine network: a cluster of associated congregations maintaining fellowship with the elder as the center. The brevity of the closing — no personal names, no detailed individual greetings as in Paul — reinforces the collective character: this is a church-to-church letter, not a personal correspondence. Eklektes (elect/chosen) — the same word as 'elect lady' in v. 1 — gives the closing symmetry: the letter moves from elect lady (v. 1) to her elect sister (v. 13), bookending the community-focus of the whole.
